data.title: XLU: Utilities Poised To Offer Blistering Returns, Strong Buy
data.description:
The utilities sector market dynamics shifted significantly in the past year as the robust demand from power-hungry data centers began to force power companies to invest hundreds of billions of dollars to enhance power generation. The bullish conditions also lifted electric utility companies expectations to earn a high single- to double-digit earnings growth over the long term.

Therefore, I believe it is the best time to initiate a stake in utilities sector to capitalize on the potential returns. Therefore, I upgrade my rating on The Utilities Select Sector SPDR® Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:XLU) to a strong buy from buy.

Data Centers And Power Consumption

After a decade of sluggish US electricity consumption, the market trends indicate a massive and prolonged rebound is ahead, which could significantly surpass the supply. While the electric cars and increasing industrial footprints are adding to demand, the power-hungry AI data centers are likely to contribute substantially to electricity demand.

Currently, the data centers account for nearly 9% of US electric consumption. According to the EIA, the data center's power consumption is expected to represent almost half of the demand growth in the next five years. In fact, the trend is expected to bolster over the long term. According to the National Electrical Manufacturers Association, the data centers energy consumption could surge by 300% in the next 10 years.

Utilities Earnings Growth Outlook Appears Promising

The sector's earnings growth forecast, a key factor for price and dividend returns, seems strong.

FactSet data shows that the utilities sector is expected to report 17% earnings growth in the third quarter and 11% in the final quarter of 2025. Moreover, the data also indicates a double digit percentage growth in the next year.

Companies in the utilities sector have also been presenting a promising earnings growth outlook with the massive investment plans to improve their power generation.

For example, NextEra Energy (NEE), the largest company in the utilities sector, reported 9% year over year earnings growth in the latest quarter.

The company stated that it expects to deliver adjusted earnings per share near the top end of its guidance of $3.63 to $4.00 and $3.85 to $4.32 in fiscal 2026 and 2027, respectively. Moreover, the company also anticipates to lift its dividends by 10% over the long term. Its share price is up 18% in the last six months.

Constellation Energy Corp's (CEG) share price rallied 77% year to date as it presented an aggressive investment plan to capitalize on power demand. It also announced a long term power purchase agreement with Meta for over 1,100 megawatts. On the other hand, Constellation expects its earnings to increase around 13% through the decade.

Duke Energy (DUK) expects to deliver 5% to 7% earnings growth over the next five years. Vistra Corp's share price soared more than 60% in the last twelve months as it aims to achieve a record adjusted EBITDA of $5.5 billion to $6.1 billion.

Vistra (VST) plans to acquire 7 natural gas facilities to improve its capacity by 2,600 megawatts. The company's share price soared 52% year to date. Other companies also seek to capitalize on growth trends while returning significant share price and dividend returns.

XLU Could be a Solid Vehicle to Ride the Long-Lasting Bull Run

There are various vehicles to access the utilities sector. In my view, exchange traded funds offer one of the best ways to capitalize on the broader market trends. Especially, the Utilities Select Sector SPDR® Fund ETF offers a cheap way to access the best companies through a single investment.

XLU's portfolio offers greater exposure to the electric utility companies, making up 65% of XLU's portfolio weight. The independent power and renewable electricity producers represent 5%, while the multi-utilities weight in its portfolio is around 25%.

XLU's portfolio comprises 34 stocks, with the top 10 accounting for 59.6%. Its portfolio includes the top-rated companies, such as NextEra Energy, Constellation Energy, Southern Co (SO), Duke Energy Corp, American Electric Power (AEP), Dominion Energy (D), Exelon (EXC) and many others. The fund's estimated earnings growth in the next 3 to 5 years is around 8.20%.

XLU's share price momentum appears strong, with a price surge of 22% so far in 2025. In addition, XLU also offers a high dividend yield of 2.5%. As the majority of companies are poised to deliver robust earnings and cash flow growth in the years ahead, the prospects of the healthy dividend hikes are also high.

Its expense ratio of 0.08% appears low compared to the median of all ETFs of 0.50%. The liquidity factor is also very strong with the $22 billion in assets under management and 11 million in average daily shares trading volume.

XLU earned a buy rating with a quant score of 3.95, according to the Seeking Alpha quant system. Its expense, liquidity, and dividend factors received an A plus grade, while a momentum factor gained a negative A grade. XLU is ranked second in its sub asset class. Moreover, XLU also appears cheap compared to the S&P 500, given its forward price to earnings ratio of 21. The fund's price to book ratio is around 2.50.

Risk Factors to Consider

Technically, XLU's standard deviation and annualized price volatility of 12.8 and 16.7% is in-line with the median of all ETFs.

The sector's performance can be impacted by regulatory challenges. This is because the majority of companies seeks to expand their power generation through investments in new projects, acquisitions, and mergers. Moreover, a lower than expected data center demand can also influence its performance. The tight electric vehicle policies and economic conditions can also dent the sector's performance.

In Conclusion

As the utilities sector is in its early stage of blistering growth trend, it might be the right time to initiate a position. The sector's valuations are also currently below the broader market index.

I believe investment vehicles, such as The Utilities Select Sector SPDR® Fund ETF, could be worthwhile investment options to consider. XLU offers a greater exposure to fast-growing electric utilities. Its expense ratio is low, price momentum is robust, dividend is strong, and liquidity is healthy.

data.title: ETHD: Don't Be Fooled By This Yield On Ether
data.description:
Details matter

How a machine works internally is what tells us what the output of the machine will be. Given that most financial instruments are indeed machines with their own internal working parts this is also true of such financial instruments. We need to grasp how those internal parts interact to gain a good idea of what the likely outcome is going to be.

This always does mean a certain amount of boring detail but there we are.

Proshares UltraShort Ether ETF (NYSEARCA:ETHD)

This is, obviously enough, an exchange traded fund. This means that it's open ended - we buy and our money goes into the fund, we sell and it comes out. This potentially could lead to a problem - if many sell, then the fund might have to dump positions in order to pay out. Such dumping - at firesale prices - then damages the value of the fund, spooking more selling, and so we get a cascade - just like a bank run. Whether this can in fact happen depends upon the liquidity of the underlying investments being made by the fund itself. Here they're in ether futures and derivatives - these are liquid enough that it's not going to be a problem.

Another feature of an ETF is that it will always trade no more than tenths of a percent from net asset value.

The aim here is to provide 2x the negative of the ether price movement. That's not been a good long term bet this past year. But it's also true that this is a trading tool, not a hold, something I'll go into in more detail. Some days this was a very good bet - or perhaps we should say day trade or speculation.

For something to day trade, the spread looks a little wide, at 0.3%. But that's more a function of that spread being a penny on a $3 to $4 stock price.

We can trade in near $100,000 lots, so there's plenty of liquidity. Actual trade is in the 60 million pieces a day range currently.

Don't be fooled by the reported yield

The real point I want to get across here - a product of that attention to the necessary details - is that we must not get fooled by the reported yield. For this looks very tempting indeed:

Yield (TTM) 242.88%

Hold for 5 months, and everything after that is house money. Which would be very exciting, yes, but that's not the way it works, of course. This is the cash dividend of the past 12 months compared to the current market price - the trailing yield. And if the market price has collapsed over the past 12 months then the sums get a little misleading. And, yes, the price has collapsed - down 93% in fact, over 12 months.

So, we're counting dividends that might have been useful or reasonable 8 and 10 months ago as against a market price down 90%. Which bumps up that trailing yield.

The actual dividends paid in 12 months are here. About $9.15 cents for the past year. At a price a year back of some $55, that's a nice enough yield - near 17%. But it's also not 242%, and it's also not really making up for the 92% fall in capital value.

To gain an idea - and it will only be an idea because the future is uncertain - of the forward yield, what we think we might get, we can just annualize the most recent payout. Which was 0.004 cents, x 12 gives us a yield of 1.4% on a $3.50 stock. No, you're right, that's not correct, but that is indicative.

So, attention to detail. Where - entirely correctly - the trailing yield is listed as 240%, that's actually nothing, at all, to do with what we might be able to expect in the future.

The other way to put this is that this isn't a dividend stock. It wasn't in the past and it's most, most, unlikely to be in the future.

The internals at ETHD

The aim is to produce 2x the bear performance of ether. This is, of course, achieved by holding derivatives:

The expense ratio is 0.99%, which for an ETF dealing in options, etc positions is reasonable enough - fairly middle of the road. But we must always remember that fees aren't the point, it's returns after fees that are. Low fees for bad returns aren't worth paying and high fees for outperformance eminently so.

The aim here?

This ProShares ETF seeks daily investment results that correspond, before fees and expenses, to -2x the daily performance of its underlying benchmark (the “Daily Target”).

All well and good but note that "daily" there. This is a short term speculative tool, not a long term investment hold. This is so much so that Seeking Alpha gives a warning about it:

For example, consider the example of a 3x leveraged ETF where the benchmark rises from 100 to 102 (+2%) on day-1, and falls from 102 back to 100 (-1.96%) on day-2. The benchmark index is back to its original level of 100, so has flat performance.

For the leveraged ETF, however, the return would be negative. To illustrate, after the first day of trading, an initial $100 investment on a 3x leveraged ETF would rise to $106.

$100 initial + (($100 x 2% index return) x 3) = $106

The next day the 3x leveraged ETF would drop 5.88% (3 x -1.96%), resulting in a value of only $99.77.

$106 - 5.88% = $99.77

Even though the benchmark index was flat, the leveraged ETF would have resulted in a loss of -0.23% over these 2 days.

$99.77 - $100 initial value = $-0.23

As does the SEC:

Leveraged and inverse ETFs typically are designed to achieve their stated performance objectives on a daily basis. Some investors might invest in these ETFs with the expectation that the ETFs may meet their stated daily performance objectives over the long term as well. Investors should be aware that performance of these ETFs over a period longer than one day can differ significantly from their stated daily performance objectives and may potentially expose investors to significant and sudden losses.

So, be warned.

As to why this is true, the costs of running a leveraged, or a bear, position include two things. One is the daily price reset. The other is that they're gaining these positions through derivatives - they have to be. But derivatives have a time value - a time value which, obviously, expires over time.

Derivatives - whether packaged into an ETF or other fund structure, or directly - are excellent ways of gaining leverage for the short term. The longer the period we try to use them, the more expensive they become. So, for longer speculative periods, do not use derivatives - nor funds themselves using derivatives.

The proof of the long term holding problem

If - and do note if! - these leveraged and bear ETFs were good long term holdings then the bull and bear versions would produce roughly opposite returns. Sure, there's be some loss from management fees but if the one was 90% down then the other, the inverse, should be 90% up. Or, at least, around and about.

So, are they?

Well, OK, the ether price has moved away from those trying to be bear. That's OK. But if we'd held the bull version over the same time - say as a pair - we should be roughly flat, right? Flat minus expenses SAY?

Ah, no, we're not, are we? If we'd held the pair over the year we'd be about 45% down. Which proves that the leveraged ETFs are not the way to hold long term positions.

Why I'm wrong

As I'm not saying do bet on ether, or don't (and you shouldn't believe me if I did. I'm the guy who said that bitcoin is over in Forbes back in 2013) then I can't be right or wrong on that idea. The price of ether is a perfectly sensible thing to speculate upon. But which way isn't something for me.

But that is where I could be wrong is that ether could fall in which case ETHD is a fine way to play that as a short term position.

My opinion

I'm fine with a portfolio containing speculative positions - would even insist that a properly balanced one should. That means that speculative - derivative and leverage based - positions on crypto are equally fine with me. They should be minimal parts of such a balanced portfolio, of course. The spice and flavour after a pension has been assured perhaps.

The use of leveraged bear ETFs like this one is to be taking a bear position intraday. That might be because we're long the underlying ETH. Or it might be just that we think there's going to be a price move - either a hedge or a naked speculation.

But this instrument - this form of instrument - just isn't how to take long term positions, either bull or bear in anything. Even if it does work - and in a couple of instruments that tactic has - greater return would have been achieved by constructing the same exposure in a different way.

Leveraged - bear - ETFs are short term trading tools and should be used that way.

And as up above, the real point of this piece is that we should not get fooled by trailing yields. We need to grasp the details of the internal of the instrument - that way we can see, again as above, that that trailing yield really is no guide at all to the forward yield.

You're absolutely correct, 243% is a very exciting yield. But that is the past 12 month cash distribution on a market price that has fallen 93%. The next 12 month distribution really isn't going to be like that at all.

data.title: Rubrik: The Cybersecurity Game-Changer That Investors Shouldn't Overlook
data.description:
Rubrik (RBRK) is a misunderstood company that may be difficult for some people to understand. The company's Co-founder and Chief Executive Officer ("CEO"), Bipul Sinha, said at the 2025 Goldman Sachs Communacopia + Technology Conference:

I'm happily dissatisfied because Rubrik will always be a misunderstood company because Rubrik will always be at the frontier of innovation. And I used to be an engineer and then I became a venture capitalist and now Founder, CEO. And I always believe that the best companies are counter consensus and misunderstood. That's when the value is created and that's when value is realized.

A significant reason people misunderstand the company is that they fail to grasp what the company does and its place in the competitive marketplace. When the company first entered the marketplace, it had disruptive data protection technology. Some investors still view it as a "backup and data recovery" platform. However, the company's CEO and management see themselves differently. Rubrik views itself as a cybersecurity company whose main product, Rubrik Security Cloud (RSC), is the last line of defense against modern threats like ransomware.

The company built this cloud product on a Zero Trust architecture ("ZTA"), which is a perimeterless cybersecurity framework that operates on the principle of never trusting, but constantly verifying every user, service, and device before granting limited access.

The company's website discusses ZTA:

To be effective, ZTA never assumes implicit trust based on a user's physical or network location. For example, just because a user is logged onto the Local Area Network (LAN), that should not entitle him or her to see any specific data available on the LAN. Nor does asset "ownership" matter. If user X is listed as the owner of a database, user X still needs to establish who they are before being allowed to access their database. ZT solutions use tools like Multi-Factor Authentication ("MFA") to authenticate users and then authorize discrete access permissions.

The company operates in multiple areas that integrate data security, risk analysis, and identity protection for use with backup data. This convergence space doesn't fit neatly into existing cybersecurity or IT infrastructure categories.

CEO Sinha believes his company has created a new cybersecurity category: cyber resilience, which is the ability of an organization to withstand and recover from cyber incidents. This ability is increasingly important in the age of artificial intelligence ("AI"). Although cybersecurity companies use AI to enhance their customers' security, this technology also empowers adversaries to create more sophisticated attacks, such as deepfakes and adaptive malware.

The company showed in its second quarter fiscal year ("FY") 2026 results that the market accepts Rubrik's "cyber resilience" value proposition through several key growth metrics.

Rubrik's subscription ARR (Annual Recurring Revenue) increased 36% year-over-year to $1.25 billion. This type of growth at scale indicates broad market adoption of its subscription-based platform, the RSC.

Subscription NRR (Net Revenue Retention) remained above 120%, indicating that the company's existing customers increased their recurring revenue by 20% over the previous year. Existing customers are not only staying but also significantly increasing their spending with Rubrik.

The company's Chief Financial Officer ("CFO"), Kiran Choudary, noted on the second quarter FY 2026 earnings call that "Adoption of additional security functionality contributed approximately 35% of our subscription net retention rate in the quarter," clear financial evidence that customers are interested in the cyber resilience concept by voting with their wallets. Customers are buying the added security layers, not just the basic backup product.

Customers with $100,000 or greater in subscription ARR increased 27% year-over-year to 2,505 customers. These larger customers increased from 82% of the total subscription ARR last year to 85% this year, indicating Rubrik is becoming a strategic partner to large enterprises, which aligns with the high-stakes, board-level issue of cyber resilience.

CFO Choudary said the following on the second-quarter earnings call:

We remain confident about our outlook given the strength of the cyber resilience market and demand for our differentiated offerings. We believe these drivers alongside our strong and consistent execution will deliver strong subscription ARR growth ahead.

As long as the market believes the company can achieve robust revenue growth, investors will award it a premium valuation, primarily because the market values the stock based on subscription ARR growth and revenue growth.

Rubrik Customer Wins

CEO Sinha detailed a customer win on the second-quarter earnings call that illustrated why the idea of cyber resilience and an "assume a breach" mindset is becoming popular in the C-suites of large companies:

This unique architecture consistently enables us to outperform both legacy and new-gen backup vendors. Let me highlight this with 2 illustrative customer wins from the quarter. A major North American oil and gas company selected Rubrik after its legacy backup provider was unable to support a fast recovery following a disruptive cyber attack. Rubrik was selected because of our superior recovery time relative to both legacy as well as new-gen alternatives. Our comprehensive yet radically simple platform for cyber recovery across all workloads, including the cloud, was another key reason for the legacy backup replacement.

The above customer win is an example of Rubrik replacing a legacy backup provider that was unable to satisfy the customer's needs. The major North American oil and gas company chose Rubrik because of its superior performance over rivals, validating Rubrik management's core message that traditional backup solutions are insufficient for modern cyber threats.

The CEO also highlighted a significant win with a Fortune 50 pharma leader:

A Fortune 50 pharma leader turned to Rubrik to protect its critical applications, displacing its 20-year-old legacy backup vendor as well as native cloud backup solutions. We also outcompeted new-gen backup vendors for this opportunity. Rubrik was selected due to not only our ability to deliver greater cyber resiliency in the face of escalating cyber risk, but also more efficient cloud storage costs.

The above customer win exemplifies Rubrik replacing a 20-year-old legacy vendor and native cloud backup solutions due to its ability to provide better cyber resiliency against rising cyber risk.

Rubrik also won in areas outside its original backup and data recovery business. The company now has an identity resilience business that it rolled out in April 2025. CEO Sinha said on the earnings call, "We are the only vendor in the market that delivers rapid recovery of both Active Directory and Entra ID in a hybrid cloud manner, the backbone of identity solutions worldwide."

Active Directory is Microsoft's on-premises directory service for managing user identities and network resources, and Entra ID is Microsoft's cloud-based ID service. The CEO highlighted two wins in identity resilience: a U.K. financial services company and a Fortune 500 financial institution. Both companies found issues with their Active Directory environment. CEO Sinha said specifically about the Fortune 500 win:

A Fortune 500 financial institution in the U.S. turned to Rubrik after an audit uncovered that its Active Directory recovery could take upwards of 7 days, with millions of dollars at risk each day. By adding Rubrik Identity Recovery, they reduced recovery times to under 2 hours, preventing potentially significant business disruption and satisfying Board mandates.

The company likely considered it essential to highlight Identity wins, as Data & Identity Survivability is a relatively new service that follows the establishment of its foundational Backup and Recovery service. The CEO wants investors to understand that Rubrik is not just a one-trick pony.

All of the above wins involve large enterprises, which is where investors want to see the wins. Large enterprise deals typically result in predictable, subscription-based ARR that is less likely to churn than smaller companies, which helps the company produce a solid NRR. Investors will often award a company with an NRR above 120% a premium valuation.

Additionally, when Rubrik serves enterprise customers, it drives economies of scale, which improves gross margins (nearly 80%). Economies of scale mean that as revenue grows, profitability increases at an even faster rate, helping the company move towards breaking even and eventually becoming profitable.

Generally, investors will award companies with increasing gross margins through economies of scale with a premium valuation.

Lastly, large enterprise wins in a highly competitive market signal that Rubrik is gaining market share against entrenched legacy vendors with its modern cloud-native platform, proving its superiority against older technology.

Valuation

Rubrik's trailing 12-month ("TTM") price-to-sales (P/S) ratio is 13.69, well above the Information Technology ("IT") median of 3.53, a sign of potential overvaluation.

The following chart compares Rubrik's TTM P/S ratio to several partial or direct competitors. Commvault Systems (CVLT) is the most similar and is a direct competitor to Rubrik in the comparison.

Although Rubrik achieved the highest quarterly year-over-year revenue growth rate of its peers, Broadcom (AVGO), Datadog (DDOG), and Zscaler (ZS) have higher P/S ratios. If investors only compare revenue growth rates, some might consider Rubrik undervalued compared to Broadcom, Datadog, and Zscaler. Some may interpret Rubrik's faster revenue growth rate over Commvault Systems (CVLT), Okta (OKTA), and NetApp (NTAP) as justifying its higher TTM P/S ratio and may not consider Rubrik overvalued compared to those companies.

The following table compares Rubrik's one-year forward P/S to the estimated revenue growth (P/S/G) ratio and trailing 12-month ("TTM") profitability figures. Generally, the market considers the company with the lowest P/SG ratio as undervalued compared to companies with higher P/S/G ratios. Given Rubrik's lowest P/S/G in the comparison, some may consider it undervalued.

Company Name One-year Forward P/S ratio One Year Forward estimated revenue growth One-year forward P/S ratio-to-estimated revenue growth TTM FCF margin TTM Gross Margin TTM Operating Margin TTM ROIC
Rubrik 13.31 39.23% 0.34 16.66% 77.96% -39.58% -----
Commvault Systems 6.95 16.87% 0.41 17.83% 81.02% 8.59% 25.12%
Broadcom 26.60 22.79% 1.16 41.59% 63.13% 39.33% 13.77%
Zscaler 15.13 22.53% 0.67 27.18% 76.82% -4.48% -1.42%
Datadog 17.20 24.03% 0.72 28.32% 79.90% -0.61% 3.00%
Okta 5.51 10.50% 0.52 30.36% 76.91% 3.00% 2.26%
NetApp 3.51 2.95% 1.19 25.15% 69.76% 21.80% 32.14%

The high quality of Rubrik's business (relatively high gross margin of 77.96%), combined with its aggressive growth (one-year forward estimated revenue growth of 39.23%, the highest in the group), makes its low P/S/G ratio of 0.34 intriguing for growth investors. The company is generating solid Free Cash Flow ("FCF") despite significant operating losses, validating its high growth strategy and suggesting that slowing research and development (R&D) spend could quickly lead to high profitability.

Rubrik's forward FY 2029 price-to-earnings (P/E) to estimated earnings-per-share ("EPS") growth ("PEG") ratio is 0.60. Generally, the market considers a PEG ratio below 1.0 as a sign of undervaluation. Suppose the company traded at an FY 2029 PEG ratio of 1.0; the stock price would be $129, a 39.41% decline from its October 14 price of $78.17.

The company's forward fiscal year ("FY") 2030 PEG ratio is 1.12, which some might interpret as a fair valuation. However, the problem with using a forward P/E ratio with this company is that analysts expect EPS to turn profitable several years from now. The further out that analysts attempt to estimate EPS, the more inaccurate the estimate, which is likely why only one analyst tried to estimate out to FY2029 and FY2030. So, take this PEG ratio analysis with a massive grain of salt.

Some might value the company using its FCF-based valuations. However, Rubrik is in the hyper-growth stage, which can make FCF-based valuations highly inaccurate. The best valuation method for Rubrik today is revenue-based valuations because the stock is speculative. Investors in the company speculate that it can sustain high revenue growth rates while eventually achieving profitability. If revenue growth slows for any reason, current investors may see downside.

Risks

Although most of the ways that I have chosen to value the stock suggest undervaluation, on an absolute basis, the stock sells at a premium P/S valuation to the IT sector and the market, suggesting that market expectations are already high. If the company's revenue forecasts disappoint, the stock could sell off.

Management's guidance indicates moderation in revenue growth for the second half of fiscal year 2026, which may concern investors who have recently seen exceptional growth. The company grew its FY 2025 annual revenue by 41.19%. As shown in the table below, analysts estimate that annual growth in this fiscal year will decline to 39.19% and further decline to 24.83% in FY 2027. Momentum growth investors will sometimes sell off stocks that show decelerating growth. The stock price could go into a steep decline if the company fails to meet or exceed the following revenue estimates.

Rubrik uses significant stock-based compensation [SBC], which dilutes shareholder value and can mask profitability in GAAP reporting. While SBC as a percentage of revenue has trended down from 51.24% in the second quarter of the previous year to 28.54% in the second quarter this year, SBC remains a significant factor that could hold back the company from achieving GAAP (generally accepted accounting principles) profitability soon. It is something for investors to monitor.

One other factor that could prevent the company from achieving profitability is competition. Rubrik faces several significant, established players like Dell Technologies (DELL) and International Business Machines (IBM) that aren't ready to concede the data management market. These companies have more resources and name recognition, which may enable them to compete vigorously with Rubrik. Additionally, it competes with smaller emerging players and startups such as Cyera, an AI-based data security startup, and Calamu, which provides data resilience by fragmenting and encrypting data to make it useless for hackers. The combination of competition from legacy players and startups may create pricing pressure that could eat into Rubrik's margins and revenue growth rate.

So far, these fears haven't deterred growth investors from investing in the company. The market appreciates the company's improving margins, accelerating revenue growth (51% year-over-year revenue increase in the second quarter of fiscal year 2026), and positive cash flow. As a result, Rubrik will likely achieve profitability, albeit most analyst forecasts suggest it is still a few years away. However, if any of the factors that favor the company today disappear, Rubrik could see multiple compression.

Generally, when a company may take a few years to achieve profitability, investors will have concerns about whether the company has enough cash to reach profitability.

The company reported $1.523 billion in cash and short-term investments. The company achieved this large cash balance through its recent initial public offering ("IPO") on April 25, 2024, and its senior convertible note issuance on June 13, 2025, for $1.15 billion aggregate principal amount of 0.00% due 2030.

Rubrik's debt-to-equity (D/E) ratio of -2.02 indicates the company has negative shareholder equity (a negative book value), which the market often considers to be bad. It means that the company's total assets are not sufficient to cover all of its liabilities.

A negative D/E can potentially put Rubrik in a precarious financial position where it becomes increasingly reliant on borrowing to fund operations, as the losses deplete the capital contributed by shareholders. Even as the company becomes more reliant on borrowing, lenders may become more reluctant to lend it money because lenders recognize that if the company were liquidated, there wouldn't be enough assets to pay off all its creditors.

Rubrik's negative equity is likely the result of accumulated losses (negative retained earnings) over time as the company invested heavily in growth, sales, and research before achieving profitability. While a negative D/E may dissuade value investors from investing, growth investors often value tech companies based on non-balance sheet metrics like future revenue growth, Annual Recurring Revenue [ARR], and market potential, rather than their current book value, especially since the company's IPO funds and recent debt issuance have given the company a cash cushion to mitigate against immediate insolvency risk.

Rubrik's annualized EBITDA for its second quarter of FY 2026 was -$322 million, and its annualized debt-to-EBITDA ratio was -3.55, which some conservative investors may consider a significant negative.

However, the good news for bullish investors is that the company is not burning cash, but is generating positive FCF; it produced $56.36 million of FCF in the second quarter of FY 2026 and $180.47 million in TTM FCF.

Given Rubrik has $1.52 billion in cash and management forecasts a positive FCF of $145 to $150 million for the full year 2026, it is highly unlikely to run into financial trouble or need to raise additional funds in the foreseeable future just to keep operating.

Rubrik Is A Buy For Growth Investors

If this company can meet high revenue growth expectations, the stock may continue higher in the short term. However, Rubrik may not be the value or risk-averse investors' cup of tea because the company constantly lives on the cutting edge of innovation, which is a riskier strategy than investing in a known, mature, stable business. Rubrik has significant execution risks and a profitability trade-off. The company's business model requires substantial investment in multiple technologies. The amount the company needs to invest to stave off competitors may prevent it from achieving profitability quickly.

CEO Sinha said on the second quarter FY 2026 earnings call:

As I noted during our IPO, Rubrik by design perpetually lives on the frontier of innovation, and our long-term success depends upon our ability to continuously create and commercialize pioneering products. As part of this, we continue to build a portfolio of innovation at different stages and at different levels of risk. This approach allows us to stack multiple S-curves to maintain maximum momentum while preparing for what's next.

An S curve used in the above context means the life cycle of a technology. The life cycle of technology typically begins slowly, followed by a period of rapid growth, and eventually slows down as the technology matures. If a company ties itself to only one technology, eventually that technology will mature, and the company's financial performance will deteriorate in concert with the technology. As a result, Rubrik invests in technology at various points of the S-curve, including pioneering technology. This strategy should help the company remain a relevant high-growth investment for a much longer period.

Betting on multiple S-curves is also a risk mitigation strategy. Although some of the technology that Rubrik invests in may be risky and fail to take off, by making numerous investments in new initiatives on the S-curve, if one fails to take off, the company has other bets in the pipeline to fall back on, making the overall business model more resilient. So, the company may have less risk than some perceive.

Each new S-curve that the company invests in targets a new or adjacent market. Rubrik's initial S-curve was for data protection (cyber recovery). The next S-curve the company invested in was Identity Resilience, which targets the identity security market. Its next move was Enterprise AI, which targets an entirely new opportunity. This strategy is a direct way to continuously expand the company's Total Addressable Market [TAM], which is crucial for the market to justify the stock's high valuation.

The company identified the size of the market it addresses in its S-1 (issued on April 1, 2024):

We believe our total addressable market opportunity for our platform will be approximately $36.3 billion by the end of calendar year 2024 and approximately $52.9 billion by the end of calendar year 2027, based on market estimates in Gartner® research, representing an average 13% compounded annual growth rate.

The company's TAM should be $41.02 billion at the end of calendar year 2025. At the end of the second quarter of FY 2026, the company generated $1.083 billion, which is 2.64% of the estimated $41.02 billion cyber resilience market and 2.05% of its 2027 TAM.

Even in a competitive market, if Rubrik continually expands its TAM, it should have plenty of room to run over the next ten years, which is part of the reason that growth investors remain interested in the stock.

This stock may appeal to aggressive growth investors who like investing in innovative technology. I rate this stock a buy for aggressive growth investors only.

data.title: 5 Dividend Compounders I'm Buying For Passive Income
data.description:
As a dividend growth investor, it sometimes feels like I live on a different planet than most other investors.

Most attention in the financial media and among the investing community is paid to rising asset prices, whether those assets are crypto, gold, silver, rare-earth minerals, or stocks associated with (so far) unprofitable AI ventures.

The air of optimism has driven stock indices like the S&P 500 (SPY) higher, pushing its forward P/E ratio to ~23x based largely on speculation about future profits from AI.

Unfortunately for dividend-oriented investors, this has pushed the SPY's dividend yield down to its lowest level since... well, since the last time the investing community was this optimistic about a revolutionary new technology.

That doesn't mean there aren't good buying opportunities for investors seeking passive income. There are. I've got five of them on my buy list right now.

But it does mean that the market is heavily bifurcated between the realm of speculative fervor and the realm of coolheaded pragmatism.

You can take a guess where the best dividend compounders are to be found today.

In what follows, I want to zoom out and try to capture a big-picture view of the US macroeconomy, holding lots of various points in mind concurrently, before moving on to some brief comments about the five tickers on my buy list right now.

Onward!

The Big Picture

Sometimes it is nice to zoom out in order to get a big-picture view of the economy in order to hold a number of major points in mind simultaneously.

While I'm probably missing something, here are seven major points to provide a big picture view of the macroeconomy right now:

  1. Low, flat, or slightly negative net immigration combined with a below-replacement fertility rate means that US population growth, which was already quite low, has taken another big step down. This will severely restrict GDP growth.
  2. There are no signs that tariffs are being absorbed by foreign suppliers on a net basis. US businesses and consumers are absorbing the full cost of tariffs, which will also dampen GDP growth.
  3. Homebuying affordability remains extremely low, leading to very low housing turnover, further restricting GDP growth.
  4. The labor market is all but frozen, as very little hiring, firing, or job-switching is being done. Rather than a cause, this is likely an effect of fundamentally low GDP growth and economic dynamism.
  5. The artificial intelligence boom and its associated infrastructure buildout are by far the biggest driver of economic growth in the US today. This buildout looks likely to continue many years into the future, even if AI-related company valuations deflate, because a large portion of it is funded from the hyperscalers' robust cash flows from non-AI businesses.
  6. Overall consumer inflation is likely to increase only slightly over the next year or so as tariffs are mostly offset by disinflation/deflation in services and housing.
  7. A lower Fed Funds rate (which logically follows from the above points) should lend some support to the real estate and banking sectors as well as small businesses and small-cap stocks.

I've discussed each of these points at various times over the last year or so, but I'd like to comment further on just the first two of these points.

Point #1: Demographics

I think a lot of people severely underestimate the importance of demographics to the economy.

Granted, productivity growth is a bigger driver of real GDP per capita, which is perhaps the primary metric to measure economic progress. But in developed nations, population growth, especially of the working-age population, has a significant and underappreciated impact on GDP growth.

After all, people are both consumers and workers. A larger population creates more aggregate demand for goods and services, which leads to more jobs and investment, which leads to more aggregate demand, and so on.

I specify "developed nations" above because in very poor and undeveloped countries, there may be limited ability to increase production to meet the aggregate demand from a growing population.

But in developed nations, falling growth in the working-age population not only weighs on aggregate demand, it also has a negative impact on productivity growth. Studies like this one show that aging populations consistently correlate with slowing productivity growth.

This is one of several ways that falling population growth or outright population decline negatively affects the economy, as I discussed recently in "Peak Humans: The Megatrend No One Is Talking About (Yet)."

So, in short, low population growth (especially for the working-age group) is negative for the economy all around.

That's why it's troubling to see the Congressional Budget Office's recent update to its long-term demographic outlook, showing US population growth declining even faster than in its last forecast.

Note that this projection is highly uncertain because the current level of net immigration is unclear. Estimates for 2025 range from a decrease of a few hundred thousand to an increase of 1 million.

Also note that in the CBO's update, the projected point at which the natural increase in the US population (births minus deaths) would turn negative moved up from the mid-2030s to the early 2030s.

In other words, assuming zero net immigration, the US population would peak in 5-7 years.

In the CBO's previous demographic projection from just earlier this year, they forecast that the US population would grow from its current ~350 million to 372 million in 2055. Now, given lower net immigration, the nonpartisan forecasters say that US population growth will be slower, and the total population is projected to reach 367 million in 2055.

Given the fact that the foreign-born population has higher rates of employment and fertility than the native-born population, lower net immigration basically translates into lower growth in the working-age population.

The US-born (or native-born) working-age population is projected to decline continuously into the future, and a low-immigration scenario (such as what we currently have) would merely hold the total working-age population flat for the next decade.

All net growth in the US working-age population in the future, if we are to have any, will come from immigration.

The upshot for the US economy as a whole, as argued in a recent study, is lower future GDP growth.

Lower net immigration means lower population growth (especially working-age), lower aggregate consumption, and lower productivity growth - all of which is another way of saying lower GDP growth.

This is the main reason why the Federal Reserve Bank of Dallas recently estimated ultra-low breakeven job growth numbers for the remainder of this year.

The breakeven rate here is the amount of monthly jobs created to keep the unemployment rate flat.

This goes a long way in explaining why such low job growth figures reported this year have not led to a significant rise in the unemployment rate.

Point #2: Who Is Really Paying For Tariffs

Now, briefly on the second point in the list above, it's worth noting that if foreigners were helping to shoulder the burden of the tariffs, we would be able to see it in the data on import prices.

Seeing as just about everything from every country of origin is being tariffed to some degree, we could just look at overall import price changes. If foreign suppliers lower their prices to absorb some of the tariff costs, we would see import prices fall.

That is not what we are seeing.

This is a bit different than the first round of tariffs in 2018-2019, when we saw an immediate adjustment in import prices when tariffs began in September 2018. Over the course of 2019, import prices gradually fell, lending some credence to the idea that foreign suppliers were indeed shouldering a portion of the tariff costs.

This year, so far, we are seeing no net decrease in import prices.

But someone has to pay those import taxes. So far, that "someone" has mainly been the US businesses importing goods, but gradually, American consumers will also absorb some portion of the tariffs as the cost gets passed through to them via price hikes.

The only other potential channel by which tariffs could be indirectly paid by foreigners is if the US dollar appreciated against foreign currencies, thereby making it effectively cheaper to buy foreign goods. The opposite has happened this year:

It's hard to escape the plain data showing that Americans are going to pay for the tariffs in one form or another.

That, in turn, will weigh down GDP growth.

5 Dividend Compounders I'm Buying Now

The above discussion tempers my expectations for earnings growth from many parts of the stock market. If it isn't somehow connected to the massive AI infrastructure spending boom or some other idiosyncratic growth trend, it will likely struggle to generate meaningful growth over the next few years.

That said, in the long run (the next several years), if AI does turn out to be the kind of bubble that bursts and causes related stock prices to deflate, then reasonably valued dividend growth stocks and REITs look like a fantastic place to invest right now.

Compare the total return performance of the Nasdaq Index (QQQ) to the Vanguard Equity Income Fund (VEIPX) and Vanguard Real Estate Fund (VGSIX) from the beginning of Q2 1999 through the end of Q2 2002:

While boring dividend compounders and REITs significantly underperformed during the Internet bubble, they massively outperformed after the bubble began to deflate.

If you're able to masterfully time the top in AI-related stock prices, by all means do so. Don't let me stop you.

But alas, your humble financial writer lacks such ability. As such, I content myself with dividend compounders, confident that they will continue to compound cash flows and dividends whether the market recognizes their value or not.

My buy list this week includes three stocks and two ETFs - dividend compounders, all.

Dividend Yield Projected Dividend Growth Rate (Guesstimate)
American Homes 4 Rent (AMH) 3.7% Mid-Single-Digit
Bar Harbor Bankshares (BHB) 4.4% Mid-Single-Digit
InvenTrust Properties (IVT) 3.4% Mid-Single-Digit
Global X MLP & Energy Infrastructure ETF (MLPX) 5.0% Low- to Mid-Single-Digit
TBG Dividend Focus ETF (TBG) 3.6% Mid- to High-Single-Digit

Allow me a few brief comments on these picks (except TBG, which I'll discuss further in a future article).

American Homes 4 Rent

First, on the subject of AMH and its portfolio of single-family rental homes, note that the economics still massively favor renting over buying.

US housing affordability is hovering near its lowest point in the past 40 years, especially for would-be first-time buyers.

This, I believe, will push a lot of Millennials into single-family rentals rather than into buying starter homes, which should maintain steady demand for AMH's properties.

Furthermore, although there is near-constant talk of how big corporate landlords are eating up the entire housing market, it's worth noting that landlords with portfolios of more than 1,000 properties represent a mere 1.6% of the total SFR market.

Regulatory threats to corporate SFR owners strike me as being low-probability risks for this reason. In most markets, regulating away big corporate landlords like AMH would make little to no difference to housing affordability.

Bar Harbor Bankshares

BHB is a New England regional bank headquartered in the touristy town of Bar Harbor, Maine, adjacent to Acadia National Park. Shudders through the regional banking sector (KRE) have caused this small-cap stock to fall in sympathy, but BHB's local real estate-focused loan portfolio lacks exposure to the kinds of things getting other lenders in trouble today.

InvenTrust Properties

I just published an article highlighting my Strong Buy thesis for IVT, and it's a short article, so rather than rehash my argument, I shall simply point you to that article.

Global X MLP & Energy Infrastructure ETF

Infrastructure Capital Advisors recently published an article highlighting the bullish case for midstream energy companies today.

In short, unlike the mid 2010s when so much midstream growth capex was funded via debt, making the industry vulnerable to an oil price crash, the midstream sector is largely self-funded and deleveraged today. Plus, there are tailwinds from AI infrastructure, which will require huge amounts of power, much of it to come from natural gas.

I like MLPX because it balances exposure to high-yielding MLPs like Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) and Energy Transfer (ET) with low-yielding but high-growth LNG players like Cheniere Energy (LNG).

data.title: Tamarack Valley: High Profit Margin, But Wading Through Too Many Risks
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I grant Tamarack Valley a hold rating (OTCPK:TNEYF) (TSX:TVE:CA). I am of the opinion that the company could face significant risks that would affect its operations. The first risk is lower oil prices. The U.S. Energy Information Administration (EIA) projects that the Brent oil price will average $51 per barrel in 2026. In addition, the WTI forward curve indicates that market participants are taking long and short positions in WTI oil futures contracts at prices per barrel in the range of $61 to $64 for the upcoming years. Based on these values, I projected Tamarack's earnings before taxes (EBT), suggesting that it will reach CA$138 million in 2029. This value is 34.15% lower than the EBT reported by the company in 2024. Secondly, the company had a reserve life index of 4.83 years, considerably lower than many of its peers.

However, Tamarack is a profitable firm. It has higher profit margins than many of its peers, since it produces heavy and light oils. In addition, its proven reserves increased by 126.20% from 2018 to 2024. Further, it has reduced its debt level, improving this aspect. Despite considering all the positive aspects, I still rate Tamarack with a hold.

The company

Tamarack is a Canadian oil and gas company headquartered in Calgary, Alberta. The company has transformed its operations. In 2020, most of its activities were concentrated in Cardium and Viking. Meanwhile, in H1 2025, Tamarack's operations were concentrated in Clearwater and Charlie Lake. In the first location, it mainly produced heavy oil, while in the latter location, it focused on producing light oil. During H1 2025, Tamarack averaged a production of 68,986 barrels of oil equivalent per day (boe/d), of which 60% was heavy oil, 21% light oil, and 19% natural gas and NGL, as depicted in the following chart.

Positive Aspects

I divided this article into two main sections. The first one, which is this one, comprises the main positive aspects of the company. Meanwhile, the second main section will focus on some of the challenges that Tamarack faces.

Heavy and Light Oil

Tamarack concentrates its operations on producing light and heavy oil. Due to this characteristic, the company has higher gross profit margins than many other Canadian oil and gas companies. With that being said, the following table shows some profit ratios of Tamarack, Athabasca Oil (OTCPK:ATHOF), Baytex Energy (BTE), Cardinal Energy (OTCPK:CRLFF), Headwater Exploration (OTCPK:CDDRF), International Petroleum (OTCPK:IPCFF), Obsidian Energy (OBE), Surge Energy (OTCPK:ZPTAF), Vermilion Energy (VET), and Whitecap Resources (OTCPK:WCPRF). The company mentions these companies as its peers. As the green cells indicate, Tamarack has the highest gross profit, EBITDA, and levered FCF margins among its peers. In addition, its EBITDA and net income margins are higher than their respective median values.

Many companies in the table above produce bitumen, which is a type of extra-heavy oil. To be transported through pipelines, this type of oil is blended with lighter oil. However, the diluents represent a significant portion of the costs. For instance, ATHOF's costs of diluent accounted for 35.70% of its revenues in 2024. Meanwhile, in the same year, Tamarack reported product purchases and blending (diluents) for CA$19.51 million, representing 1.45% of its revenues. For this reason, Tamarack has a higher profit margin, to some extent.

Breakeven Price

In Q2 2025, the WTI oil price averaged $64.57 per barrel. That quarter, the company reported a net income before taxes margin of 33.13%. To add another perspective, the company indicates that it can cover its costs and pay dividends even if the WTI oil price is below $40 per barrel.

In a report of the EIA, oil producers in the Permian Midland Basin had a breakeven price of $62 per barrel in 2024. Meanwhile, in the Permian Delaware Basin, the breakeven price was $64 per barrel. These are the two largest basins in the Permian. The Permian Basin accounted for 48% of the U.S. oil production in 2024. Additionally, in the U.S., most of the growth in oil and natural gas production is expected to come from the Permian Basin. As such, if oil prices decline below $60, many oil producers in the Permian Basin will reduce their oil production or reduce their capex. Consequently, if the previous scenario occurs, it will reduce the global oil supply, which will ultimately push oil prices up. As such, Tamarack will be in a better position than many oil producers in the Permian Basin.

Oil Reserves

I think that an excellent indication of any oil and gas company is its proved reserves. The more reserves a company has, the more years it can extract oil. The following table shows that Tamarack had 50 million barrels of oil equivalent (MMboe) of net proved reserves in 2018, increasing to 113 MMboe in 2024, representing a 126.20% increase. As such, the company is in a better position.

.

Tamarack has expanded its operations through mergers and acquisitions. In 2020, it completed two strategic acquisitions in Clearwater. With these transactions, the company increased its production by 2,000 boe/d.

In 2022, the company acquired Deltastream Energy Corporation, adding 13.4 MMboe of proved developed reserves and 51.0 MMboe of proved and probable reserves to its operations. The assets are located in Clearwater. In addition, in Q2 2025, Tamarack acquired assets in Clearwater for CA$51.5 million, adding more than 114 net sections of Clearwater lands.

Debt

There are companies with wonderful businesses, but they often experience financial distress or bankruptcy due to inadequate debt management. As such, I always try to include a small section to evaluate the debt position of my write-up.

In Q2 2025, Tamarack only had CA$2.76 million of cash and cash equivalents, and CA$174.04 million of current assets. In the same period, it reported 172.47 in current liabilities. As such, its current liabilities and current assets are similar. However, the company had CA$717.40 million in long-term debt, considerably higher than its current assets.

To reduce its debt, the company sold non-core assets in the Veteran Consort and Eyehill areas, located in Eastern Alberta, Canada. This operation is for CA$112 million before closing adjustments. With these resources, the company expects to reduce its debt. Tamarack expects to lower its debt to CA$500 million by 2027, which is a promising prospect.

Negative Aspects

Up to this point, I have discussed only the positive aspects of Tamarack. However, this article will be incomplete if I do not discuss some of the potential risks that could undermine Tamarack's operations.

Energy Outlook and Projection

The EIA expects lower oil prices for 2025 and 2026 compared to 2024, which is negative for Tamarack. The institution projects that the Brent oil price will average $67 per barrel in 2025 and $51 per barrel in 2026, as depicted in the following chart. If the Brent oil price declines to $51 in 2026, Tamarack could face a similar situation to the one observed in 2016 and 2017.

The company's performance is closely tied to oil production, since most of Tamarack's revenues are derived from oil. In its 2025 Investor Day, Tamarack projects that the WTI oil price will average $75 for 2025-2029. However, as the previous chart indicates, the EIA projections suggest lower oil prices for this year and 2026. To add context, the Brent oil price tends to trade at a higher price than the WTI oil price. As such, the EIA projections point out that the WTI oil price would average less than $51 per barrel in 2026. For projection purposes, I will assume that the WTI oil price will average $49 in 2026, which is $2 lower than the Brent oil price.

For 2027-2029, I anticipate that the WTI will average $62 per barrel. This value is taken from the WTI forward curve, as depicted below. The forward curve shows the future market perspective of the oil price. The chart indicates that market participants are taking long and short positions in the range of $61-$64 per barrel of WTI futures contracts for the upcoming years. Since the EIA only implements projections for this year and the next, I think that using the WTI forward curve to project the WTI oil price is a reasonable assumption.

Hitherto, I have only discussed the WTI oil price. Albeit I have not projected Tamarack's oil production. As such, the following paragraph of this section will project the production and revenues of the company.

For this year, Tamarack expects to produce between 65,000 boe/d and 67,000 boe/d. At the midpoint of this expectation, production is expected to increase by 2.59% annually in 2025. For 2029, the company expects to produce approximately between 75,000 and 80,000 boe/d (page 6 of Tamarack's June 2025 investor day presentation). Assuming that the company produces 75,000 boe/d in 2029, it will represent a 16.58% increase compared to 2024. I use 75,000 boe/d, the lower band, to be conservative with my projection.

With the projection presented in the previous paragraph, it is possible to derive Tamarack's growth outlook. Assuming that Tamarack's revenues per boe will move in the same proportion as the WTI, the revenues of Tamarack will be the sum of the expected oil price growth and the company's production growth, as depicted in the following table. For instance, in 2025, my projection assumes that Tamarack's revenues will decline by 8.52%. This rate is the sum of the expected production growth and the expected oil price growth for that year (2.59% - 11.11% = -8.52%). The estimation suggests that the company's revenues would be CA$933 million in 2026. Meanwhile, according to my forecast for 2029, the revenues are expected to be CA$1,321 million.

Estimation

This section is the continuation of the previous section. I separated the previous section from this one to facilitate the comprehension of the article.

As depicted in the following table, from 2022 to the first half of 2025 (H1 2025), the total costs before taxes that Tamarack incurred to produce a boe have fluctuated from CA$39 to CA$49. In H1 2025, these costs were CA$41.81 per boe. I will assume that these costs will remain constant at CA$41.81 per boe for 2025-2029. Note that the previous figure is closer to CA$39 (the lower band). As such, my projection could overestimate Tamarack's costs.

With the revenues per boe (projected in the previous section) and the total costs before taxes per boe, it is possible to project the EBT of the company per boe. For 2026, my estimation suggests that Tamarack will generate a negative EBT of 4.61 per boe, as depicted in the table below. I assumed that the WTI would average $49 per barrel in 2026. For that reason, the company is projected to generate financial losses that year. The EBT for the full year (2026) is expected to be a loss of CA$116 million. Meanwhile, the company is expected to generate an EBT of CA$138 million in 2029. This value is 34.15% lower than the EBT reported by the company in 2024.

From 2024 to 2029E, my estimations suggest that Tamarack would reach an EBT of CA$711 million. The current market capitalization is approximately CA$2,920 million. Consequently, due to the expected low oil prices, the expansion plan does not seem to be promising for the company.

Reserve Life Index

Despite the fact that Tamarack has increased its oil reserves, its reserve life index is still relatively low. In 2024, the company had 113,373 Mboe of proved reserves, and a reserve life index of 4.83 years. In other words, the company will have to invest significant resources in exploration and acquisition of new assets to sustain its production.

There are Canadian oil companies with a higher life index than Tamarack. For instance, Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ) had a total proved reserve life index of 33 years in 2024. That year, WCPRF reported a total proved reserve life index of 12.5 years. Considering that the company has virtually no liquid assets, long-term debt of CA$717.40 million, and expects to reduce its long-term debt to CA$500 million, I think the company will have difficulties acquiring new assets in the short term.

Peer Analysis

Up to this point, I have compared Tamarack's profitability ratios to those of its peers. However, I have not included other critical metrics. This section conducts a peer analysis, including other crucial metrics, among Tamarack, ATHOF, BTE, CRLFF, CDDRF, IPCFF, OBE, ZPTAF, VET, and WCPRF. The following table shows the ratios to analyze.

In the last year, Tamarack's stock price increased by 53.87%, the highest increase among its peers. In addition, its five-year stock return was 747.15%, higher than the median return in that period. Overall, the company has had an excellent performance in the financial market among its peers.

Moving to the valuation ratios, Tamarack has ratios relatively similar to those reported by its peers. For example, it has a 1.37 price-to-book ratio, while the median price-to-book ratio is 1.245. The price-to-cash flow ratio is the only metric that suggests a considerable undervaluation.

On the other hand, the growth ratios indicate that Tamarack will face headwinds for this year. The analyst consensus expects that the company's revenues will decrease by 3.84%, lower than the median ratio. Nonetheless, the company achieved a 40.50% CAGR in its revenues over the last five years, which is an excellent value.

Finally, the debt ratio suggests that the company has achieved results comparable to those reported by its peers. Tamarack has a debt-to-equity of 35.04%, while the median ratio is 32.20%, relatively similar values. Meanwhile, its current ratio is slightly higher than that reported by its peers.

I think that Tamarack exhibits financial ratios relatively similar to those of the median ratios in the peer group. The valuation ratios are similar to their respective median values. The debt ratios of Tamarack are also similar to their respective median values. Therefore, I think that the company should have a similar performance to the theoretical median company in the peer group.

Conclusion

In light of the analysis presented in this article, I believe that the company is not likely to experience excellent performance in the upcoming years. As such, I rate the company with a hold rating. Oil prices have declined compared to 2024. At the current price, the company will not generate the same resources that it generated before. My estimation suggests that the EBT will be CA$138 million in 2029, 34.15% lower than the EBT reported by the company in 2024. This estimation assumes a $62 per WTI oil barrel in 2029, and anticipates that the annual oil production will increase by 20.47% from 2024 to 2029E.

A positive aspect is that Tamarack is more profitable than its peers, and it also surpasses many oil companies in the Permian basin. As such, the company does not show symptoms of financial distress. It recently sold some of its non-core assets to reduce its debt level. To conclude, despite some positive aspects, I think that the current low oil price context will affect the company's revenues and earnings. As my projections suggest, the company will not have excellent profit margins for the foreseeable future, supporting my hold rating

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Our Top 10 High Growth Dividend Stocks - October 2025
data.description:
Note: The stocks shortlisted and highlighted in this article are not buy recommendations per se but rather candidates for further research. Before making any investments, please use due diligence, considering your personal goals and risk tolerance. Also, some sections in the article may be repetitive from month to month for the benefit of the new readers. Such sections would be displayed in "italics," and regular readers could skip them. However, we recommend that first-time readers refer to our BLOGPOST here to get a detailed explanation of our selection methodology/process and additional criteria for Dividend Growth Stocks.

Introduction:

Every month, we start with about 400 to 500 dividend-paying stocks and use our proprietary and unique filtering criteria to bring down the number of selections to a small subset of about 20 stocks. Generally, we end up with stocks that pay dividend yields in the range of 1% to 1.5%, which is in line with what the S&P 500 (SP500) pays as well. These 20 stocks are from diverse sectors and industries, but they all have one aspect in common. They appear to be in their period of hypergrowth with great momentum. As a final step, we analyze each of the 20 stocks and use our judgment to come up with a final list of just 10 stocks. The majority of our selections (if not all) have a market cap of $10 billion or more.

Who Should Follow This Series?

The stocks highlighted in this series are for investors who are in their accumulation phase and are more interested in growing their capital at a rapid pace rather than their current income. The focus of the selections is dividend growth and growth sustainability rather than high current yield. In that sense, the risk profile of these stocks is generally higher, and they are not suitable for highly conservative investors or investors who need high current incomes to sustain their lifestyle.

Market Outlook:

The market has been making new all-time highs on the heels of the Fed's first rate cut in almost a year and expectations of a couple more rate cuts in 2025. Right now, it seems nothing matters to the market except the interest rates. The U.S. Govt has been in shutdown for nearly two weeks now, but that hasn’t really impacted the market. When President Trump suddenly announced fresh 100% tariffs on China last Friday, markets were shocked for a day, but they recovered most of the losses on the following Monday. So, unless there is really some kind of shock, the sentiment is pretty strong and likely to remain so for some time. The only thing that can upset the sentiment is if the Fed does not deliver on further rate cuts.

As the housing market, as well as the health of the broader economy, will depend greatly on what the Fed will do or not do in the coming months, you can see the current odds of the Fed's next rate cut here on the CME FedWatch tool website.

All that being said, we can never be sure of the future direction of the markets. We intend to stay invested in stocks that have the best chance of growing rapidly in a bull market and not losing as much in a down market. The purpose of this series of articles is to highlight an outstanding list of high-growth stocks that also happen to have positive momentum behind them.

For more details and background information on this series, please read our blog post (the link is provided at the beginning of the article).

Selection Criteria for High-Growth Dividend Stocks:

This section briefly describes our selection criteria and process. For detailed information, please refer to the blog post (link provided earlier). We define our basic selection criteria here to bring our target list to roughly 400 stocks. Based on these criteria, we also calculate a "Dividend Safety Quality Score." In brief, the Quality Score is derived using the following factors:

  • Current Yield
  • Dividend growth history (number of years of dividend growth):
  • Payout ratio - Preferably based on Free Cash Flow.
  • Past five-year and 10-year dividend growth
  • Chowder number - the sum of the 5-yr dividend growth rate and the current yield
  • EPS growth (average of the previous five years of growth and expected next 3-5 years' growth)
  • Debt/equity and Debt/asset ratios
  • S&P's credit ratings (Standard & Poor's Global Ratings)
  • Distance from 52-week high (current price minus 52-week high price)
  • Sales or Revenue growth for the past five years.

Additional Criteria for Dividend Growth Stocks

We will now use the following additional criteria to filter out stocks that would fit the mold of high-growth DGI stocks.

  • The payment ratio (on a cash-flow basis or EPS basis) is less than 95%, but preferably less than 80%.
  • 5-year annualized dividend growth (or 10-year growth rate) is at least 7.5% or greater. However, some exceptions can be made.
  • Chowder number (5-year dividend growth rate plus the current dividend yield) >= 9.

Until this stage of filtering, we kept the base criteria loose enough to allow a wide variety of stocks to make the list. However, we will perform additional filtering in the next stage to get the best candidates.

Most growth investors appreciate how important the earnings growth (earnings per share - EPS) is for a stock to grow its dividend rapidly. Without growth in earnings, the company cannot grow its dividends sustainably for long. So, our focus ought to be on earnings growth.

In our spreadsheet, we will add four more columns of data for each of the stocks:

  • EPS (earnings per share) Quality Rating
  • Last Qtr EPS change % % (actual)
  • Current Qtr EPS change % (est.)
  • Current year EPS change % (est.).

We will now assign weights to these four sets of data for each stock and add them to the original "Dividend Safety Quality Score" to come up with a modified Quality Score tilted in favor of high dividend growth stocks. We will call this column "High-Growth Quality Score."

Narrowing Down the List To 20 Stocks

The criteria listed above provide us with a list of roughly 350-400 stocks to work with. We will now filter it down and select roughly 50 stocks based on the following methodology.

  • We first select the top 20 stocks based on the highest High Growth-Quality-Score (adjusted for sector or industry over concentration). Please see Table-1A below.
  • We then take the top 10 stocks based on the highest 5-year dividend growth. Please see Table-1B below.
  • We will also select the top 10 stocks based on the highest 10-year dividend growth. Please see Table-1C below.
  • Finally, we will select the top 10 stocks based on the average of EPS Rating (earnings per share) and RS rating (Relative Strength). (EPS and RS ratings are taken from IBD; subscription required). Please see Table-1D below.

Table-1A: Top 20 Highest HG-Quality-Score Stocks:

Table-1B: Top 10 Highest Past Dividend Growth Stocks (5 years):

Table-1C: Top 10 Highest Past Dividend Growth Stocks (10 years):

Table-1D: Top 10 Stocks with Highest EPS and RS Rating:

We will now combine these 50 stocks from the above four tables and remove any duplicates from this combined list, as some stocks may have qualified based on multiple criteria.

The following stocks appeared two times: APH, CI, CTRA, FIX, MLI, RL, and SAN (7 duplicates).

Appeared three times: AVGO, HWM, LRCX (6 duplicates).

We are now left with 37 (50 - 13) names.

We will also remove any names where the revenue growth (over the past five years) has been negative. There is none on the list.

We will now calculate the average of EPS and RS (relative strength) ratings and remove any names that have an average of less than 60. This removes LEN, OTIS, OVV, CI, CARR, AMH, CTAS, CTRA, DKS, MSCI, and IBN. This leaves us 26 names (37-11).

We will also remove any names with both the EPS and RS less than 80. There is none. We still have 26 names.

Next, we will remove any stock that has an RS rating of less than 50. We remove TS and FSV. After removing these two names, we are left with 24 names.

Finally, we will need to ensure that there are not too many stocks from the same industry segment (or even a sector). In cases where there is overconcentration, we will keep the best ones that also have some momentum and tailwind. During this process, we prioritize the stocks that have higher Relative Strength and EPS ratings.

Finance-Bank-major:

Finance-Regional Banks: We keep SAN and BK but remove ZION.

Financial Services and Misc.: We keep MS and COF but remove VOYA.

Computer Software: We will keep MSFT.

Computer Electronics: We will keep APH but remove KLAC.

Computer- Semiconductors: We keep AVGO and LRCX but remove TSM.

Industrial- Aerospace & Defense: We keep HWM.

Industrial- Const. & Engineering: We keep PH and MLI.

Utility: We keep NFG.

In all, we removed four names at this stage, so we are finally left with 20 (24-4) names, which are presented in the table below (in order of the sector):

(FIX), (SAN), (BK), (MSFT), (TPR), (RL), (EME), (APH), (COF), (MS), (HWM), (HIG), (LLY), (AL), (PH), (RGLD), (MLI), (LRCX), (AVGO), and (NFG).

Table 2: Top 20 High-Growth DGI Stocks of the Month:

Final Step: Selection of Top 10 High-Growth DGI Stocks

Until now, we have mostly used our rule-based filtering criteria to come to a list of 20 stocks. However, we carefully avoided the high concentration of any one sector or industry segment. Now, this final step of bringing down the number of selections to just ten stocks is mostly a subjective process based on our judgment. We try to keep the group as diverse as possible, representing many sectors and industry segments. We encourage readers to look at the wider selection of 20 stocks and see if they come up with anything different and appropriate for their goals. Nonetheless, we describe below how we go about selecting these ten stocks for the month.

We sort our list of 20 stocks by sector and industry segment, then in decreasing order of high-growth quality score. We try to select one stock from each industry segment from top to bottom; however, at times, multiple stocks could appear from the same sector. We will try to keep the best stock in each industry segment based on multiple factors, such as the quality score and the recent momentum. We also need to recognize that this is "not" a permanent buy-and-hold portfolio, and some stocks get reshuffled every month, so the focus is on selecting stocks that are likely to do well on a short- to medium-term basis. That's why we are also going to give priority to stocks with solid EPS ratings and high positive momentum (Relative Strength).

We want to caution that valuation is not a factor in the selection of these stocks, so some of these may be highly overvalued. Due to high valuation, their yields tend to be on the lower side as well, in the range of 1% to 2%.

Here are our top 10 selections for this month.

Current Month Selections (Oct. 2025):

(FIX), (SAN), (TPR), (EME), (APH), (HWM), (AL), (RGLD), (MLI), and (AVGO).

Previous Month Selections:

(FIX), (SAN), (RL), (EME), (APH), (HWM), (MLI), (WPM), (AVGO), and (NFG).

Since our methodology is largely based on a filtering process, we would like to point out that once a stock has appeared on our list, it may repeat for at least a couple of months. On average, we see about 30% to 40% turnover from one month to another, but it can vary greatly. We are retaining seven out of 10 stocks from the previous month, and three stocks were replaced with new ones.

Note: Please note that if a stock was in a previous month's list but is no longer selected in the current list, it does not mean the stock is no longer a good choice. If the stock still scores an HG-Quality score of >70, we think it could still be at least a "hold" (if not a "buy"). However, for new selections, we take into account many more factors than just the HG-Quality score. The purpose here is to highlight the top candidates every month. Please conduct further research and due diligence before making buy or sell decisions.

Table 3: (High Growth DGI)

Performance

After 8.6%, 8.8%, 12.5%, 5.8%, and 7.1% gains (successively) in the previous five months, the portfolio growth slowed down this past month. But it still outperformed the S&P 500.

Backtesting, in this case, may not be highly useful, but it does give some rough idea of how an HG portfolio (as described in this article) should perform in comparison to our benchmarks, the S&P 500 and Vanguard Dividend Appreciation Index Fund (VIG). We are presenting the backtesting from January 2012 until Aug. 2025. The assumption is that our model portfolio was invested equally in ten stocks and rebalanced every year.

It is almost certain that we would not have picked the same set of stocks 12 years ago. So, this is more of an academic exercise and should be viewed with a grain of salt. Also, past performance is no guarantee of any kind for future returns.

Chart-1:

Performance Since Jan. 2023:

Since the inception of this series, we have provided the progress and performance of the series. We compare the performance of the portfolio with the benchmark fund, Vanguard Dividend Appreciation ETF, and also with the S&P 500. We provide the cumulative figures from the start date of January 2023 until the current month. Obviously, the performance will vary from month to month. Also, the performance of benchmarks may not match exactly with a buy-and-hold portfolio, as we take the performance from a specific start date and end date every month to match our 10-HG portfolio.

Table-3A:

Concluding Remarks

After five months of a very strong showing, the performance this month slowed down quite a bit. The portfolio gained roughly 2.5%, but it was still ahead of the S&P 500. The S&P 500 and our benchmark ETF, VIG, gained 1.06% and 0.75% in the same period, respectively. The star performers for this past month were Comfort Systems USA (FIX) and EMCOR Group (EME). Stocks such as Amphenol (APH), Howmet Aerospace (HWM), and Wheaton Precious Metals (WPM) performed reasonably well. However, we are replacing WPM with Royal Gold (RGLD). Also, Ralph Lauren (RL) is being replaced with Tapestry (TPR). We are also introducing/adding Air Lease (AL).
